did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid AI approach for early skin cancer detection. It extracted deep features from pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4) and combined them with handcrafted radiomic features (LBP, GLDM, GLSZM, Color Histograms). These fused features were then classified using machine learning models, specifically Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a hybrid computer-aided diagnostic system for early skin cancer detection. It details specific methodologies including watershed segmentation, feature extraction (radiomics and CNNs), and classification using Random Forest, SVM, and ANN. The study reports original experimental results, including performance metrics (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and ablation studies on the ISIC 2019 dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integrating features of radiomics and CNN models for early skin cancer detection based on watershed segmentation
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid computer-aided diagnostic system for early skin cancer detection by integrating handcrafted radiomic features with deep learning representations from pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks. The methodology involved preprocessing dermoscopic images using watershed segmentation and mean filtering, followed by the extraction of radiomic features (LBP, GLDM, GLSZM, Color Histograms) and deep features from MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4. These fused feature vectors were classified using Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, and Artificial Neural Network models. The primary evaluation was conducted on the ISIC 2019 dataset, with generalization tested on the external HAM10000 dataset. The MobileNetV2 + radiomics + Random Forest model achieved the best performance on ISIC 2019, yielding an accuracy of 94.7%, an AUC of 85.09%, and specificity exceeding 99.2%. The model also demonstrated robust generalization on the HAM10000 dataset with an accuracy of 96.3% and an AUC of 91.66%, confirming its potential for clinical triage.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a high-risk malignant lesion. Classified with high accuracy in hybrid models (e.g., 94.3-94.4% accuracy in MobileNetV2+RF on ISIC 2019; 90.8% in HAM10000 generalization). Confusion noted with Nevi and Benign Keratosis.
Studied as a benign lesion. Classified with high accuracy (e.g., 98.1% in MobileNetV2+RF on ISIC 2019; 99.1% in HAM10000). Frequently confused with Melanoma and Benign Keratosis.
Studied as a severe malignant lesion. Classified with high accuracy (e.g., 96.1% in MobileNetV2+RF on ISIC 2019). Confusion noted with Benign Keratosis and Dermatofibroma.

Studied as a benign condition. Classified with high accuracy (e.g., 89.3% in MobileNetV2 CNN alone; 90.3% in EfficientNet-B4 CNN alone). Frequently confused with Melanoma, Nevus, and Actinic Keratosis.
Studied as a premalignant lesion. Lower classification performance in CNN-only models (e.g., 71.1% accuracy in MobileNetV2). Misclassified as Melanoma, Nevi, and BKL.

Studied as a severe malignant lesion. Challenging class with lower performance in CNN-only models (e.g., 56.3% accuracy in MobileNetV2). Hybrid models showed improved performance (e.g., 79.3% AUC in EfficientNet-B4+RF).
Studied as a benign lesion. Consistently difficult to classify with low accuracy in CNN-only models (e.g., 29.2% in MobileNetV2). Frequently confused with Benign Keratosis and Melanoma.
Studied as a lesion type. Classified with moderate accuracy in CNN-only models (e.g., 50% accuracy in MobileNetV2). Included in HAM10000 generalization results.

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic image diagnostic categories (skin lesion classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into eight distinct skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Actinic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Vascular). The study evaluates hybrid models (combining radiomic features and CNN features) using Random Forest, SVM, and ANN classifiers. Performance is measured using accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and precision for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset aggregating 25,331 high-resolution dermoscopic images sourced from leading tertiary care centers, acquired using diverse dermoscopy devices (e.g., Heine, Canfield). It is part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive.

use.training: Used for model development. 80% of the dataset was allocated for training and validation via a 5-fold cross-validation strategy. Within each fold, 80% of the training subset was used for model training.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter selection and model validation. Within each fold of the 5-fold cross-validation, 20% of the training subset was used for validation.
use.testing: Used for independent testing. The remaining 20% of the ISIC-2019 dataset was used for independent testing.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: An external dataset used to test the generalization of the proposed models. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.testing: Used for external generalization testing to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to select hyperparameters for machine learning classifiers to improve classification accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
Used as a fundamental evaluation tool to assess classification system performance, quantifying true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives across all dataset categories.
Used to evaluate model performance by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) against False Positive Rate (FPR) and calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
Used to isolate and quantify the contribution of individual components (radiomics only, CNN only, hybrid fusion, and segmentation) to the overall model performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid framework combining pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4) for feature extraction with handcrafted radiomic features (LBP, GLDM, GLSZM, Color Histograms), classified by Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN).
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4 were used as pre-trained models for deep feature extraction.

EfficientNet-B4.backbone_type: MBConv blocks with residual connections and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules

architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of radiomic feature vector (R^50) and deep feature vector (R^1280 or R^1792) without weighting or dimensionality reduction.

training_details.classifier: Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network (ANN)
training_details.interpretability_method: Grad-CAM applied to auxiliary end-to-end models created by adding a new fully connected layer to the pre-trained CNNs and fine-tuning on ISIC 2019.

classification_or_output_head.details: The CNNs functioned exclusively as feature extractors. The final classification was performed by RF, SVM, or ANN on the fused feature vector. For the Grad-CAM visualization, an auxiliary single fully connected classification layer was added to the CNNs.

preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement using 5x5 Average filter (smoothing) and 3x3 Laplacian filter (sharpening/edge enhancement)
Hair removal using the DullRazor technique (morphological closure for detection and bilinear interpolation for recoloring)

Watershed segmentation for lesion isolation (involving grayscale conversion, Sobel gradient magnitude computation, adaptive thresholding for background markers, Euclidean distance transform for foreground markers, and morphological closing)
Data augmentation including horizontal and vertical flipping, rotation (±15°), zooming, and color transformations (brightness, contrast, saturation variation)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flipping, rotation (± 15°), zooming, scaling, and cropping.

Targeted expansion of minority classes to achieve approximate balance (~8000 images per class). Specific repetition counts: Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular (VASC) expanded by 51 synthetic variants each; Actinic Keratoses (AKIEC) expanded 13 times; Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) enhanced 19 times; Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) augmented 3 times; Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL) augmented 4 times; Melanoma (MEL) dataset doubled in size; Nevi (NV) left unaltered.

conclusions: Hybrid models that integrate handcrafted radiomic features (GLSZM, GLDM, CH, LBP) with deep convolutional neural network (CNN) features yield superior diagnostic performance compared to models using only radiomic or only CNN features.

The MobileNetV2 + radiomics + Random Forest (RF) configuration achieved the best overall performance on the ISIC 2019 dataset, with an average AUC of 85.09%, accuracy of 94.7%, sensitivity of 84.53%, and specificity exceeding 99.2%.

The integration of radiomic features with MobileNetV2 significantly improved performance relative to CNN-only baselines, increasing AUC by +6.85, accuracy by +3.40, and sensitivity by +12.53 while maintaining high specificity.

The proposed hybrid model demonstrated robust generalization on the external HAM10000 dataset, achieving an overall accuracy of 96.3% and an AUC of 91.66%, confirming it learned robust features rather than memorizing dataset-specific artifacts.
The model maintained exceptionally high specificity (98.90%) on the HAM10000 dataset, which is critical for clinical triage to minimize false positives and unnecessary interventions.

Watershed segmentation materially improves discrimination; removing it from the hybrid pipeline reduced AUC by approximately 4–4.3 points and specificity by approximately 5 points due to background leakage degrading the signal-to-noise ratio.

While MobileNetV2 provided the best overall balance, EfficientNet-B4 + radiomics + RF showed better performance for specific classes, such as Squamous Cell Carcinoma (79.3% AUC), indicating architecture-dependent complementarity.
Among models using only radiomic features, the RF classifier achieved an AUC of 81.3% and accuracy of 92.1%, but this was significantly outperformed by the hybrid approaches.

The study highlights that CNNs may not fully encapsulate clinically meaningful handcrafted descriptors (texture, color, border irregularity), and their fusion addresses this methodological gap to create a more comprehensive representation of lesions.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance by artificially expanding minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma, Vascular, SCC) via augmentation to achieve approximate balance (~8000 images per class), which may introduce synthetic bias or overfitting to augmented patterns.

Performance on rare or visually ambiguous classes remains low in CNN-only baselines (e.g., Dermatofibroma accuracy 29.2% in MobileNetV2; Squamous Cell Carcinoma accuracy 56.3%), indicating persistent diagnostic challenges for these subtypes despite hybrid improvements.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for performance comparisons; results are based on descriptive metrics and ablation studies.

External validation was performed on the HAM10000 dataset, showing robust generalization (96.3% accuracy, 91.66% AUC), but the specific distribution and size of the HAM10000 test set were not detailed in the provided context.
Watershed segmentation was found to be critical; its removal degraded AUC by ~4–4.3 points and specificity by ~5 points due to background leakage, highlighting a dependency on preprocessing quality.
The fusion method concatenates radiomic and deep feature vectors without dimensionality reduction or weighting, which may lead to feature dominance by the higher-dimensional CNN features.

The model achieved exceptionally high specificity (>99.2% on ISIC 2019, 98.90% on HAM10000), which is clinically favorable for triage to minimize false positives, but sensitivity for melanoma was 84.53% (ISIC 2019), suggesting room for improvement in early detection recall.

Data leakage risk exists if the augmentation or preprocessing steps were not strictly applied within the cross-validation folds, though the paper states 5-fold cross-validation was used for hyperparameter selection.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset for model development and internal evaluation, employing a 5-fold cross-validation strategy for hyperparameter tuning and model selection, with a held-out 20% subset for independent internal testing. The generalization capability of the best-performing model (MobileNetV2 + radiomics + RF) was evaluated on the external HAM10000 dataset. Pre-trained weights for MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4 were derived from ImageNet. Data augmentation was applied to the training folds to address class imbalance.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The model was trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset (using 5-fold cross-validation for tuning and a held-out 20% for internal testing) and subsequently tested on the external HAM10000 dataset to evaluate generalization to unseen data.

training.counts: 80% of the 25,331 images (approx. 20,265 images) allocated for training and validation via 5-fold CV; within each fold, 80% of the training subset was used for model fitting.
training.notes: Used for model development. Data augmentation (flipping, rotation, zooming, color transforms, and class-specific expansion) was applied within the training folds to balance classes.

validation.counts: 20% of the training subset within each of the 5 folds (approx. 4,053 images total across folds, but used for tuning, not final reporting).
validation.notes: Used for hyperparameter selection and model validation during the 5-fold cross-validation process.

testing.notes: Used for independent internal testing to report final performance metrics on the primary dataset.

external_testing.counts: Not reported (total count of images used in the external test set is not specified in the text).
external_testing.notes: Used as an unseen external dataset to test generalization. The model was not retrained on this dataset.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Pre-trained weights for MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4 were used to extract deep features. The specific ImageNet weights were not retrained on the skin cancer data but served as the feature extractor backbone.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation included horizontal/vertical flipping, rotation (±15°), zooming, and color transformations. Class balancing augmentation was applied to minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma, Vascular, SCC) to achieve approximate balance (~8000 images per class).

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 5-fold cross-validation for training/validation. A separate held-out 20% of the ISIC 2019 dataset was used for independent internal testing.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 5-fold cross-validation. Folds stayed within the ISIC 2019 dataset. Hyperparameters were selected using this CV.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The HAM10000 dataset was used as unseen external data for generalization testing. The specific size and class distribution of the HAM10000 subset used were not reported.

dataset_level_results.notes: Best performing hybrid model on the internal test set. Specificity exceeded 99.2%.
Showed better performance for specific classes like Squamous Cell Carcinoma compared to MobileNetV2.
Baseline CNN-only performance. Dermatofibroma accuracy was low (29.2%).
Baseline CNN-only performance. Dermatofibroma accuracy improved to 56.3%.
Performance of radiomic features alone with RF classifier.
Generalization results on unseen external data. Specific count of images in this test set was not reported.

cross_dataset_experiments.notes: The model trained on ISIC 2019 was applied to the HAM10000 dataset without retraining to evaluate generalization to unseen data.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: 5-fold cross-validation for training/validation; 20% held-out for internal testing.

generalizability_notes: The study demonstrates robust generalization on the external HAM10000 dataset, suggesting the model learned robust features rather than memorizing ISIC 2019 artifacts.
Performance on rare classes (Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) remained challenging even with hybrid models, indicating persistent diagnostic difficulties for these subtypes.
The study highlights a dependency on watershed segmentation; removing it degraded performance, suggesting potential sensitivity to preprocessing quality.
Data leakage risk exists if augmentation was not strictly confined to training folds, though the paper states 5-fold CV was used for hyperparameter selection.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals were reported for performance comparisons.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images in the HAM10000 external test set.
Class distribution of the HAM10000 external test set.
Specific counts of images in the internal test split (20% of 25,331 is approx. 5,066, but exact number not explicitly stated).
P-values for statistical significance of performance differences.
